stem:
A 22-month-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department at 2 a.m. by his parents who describe a barking cough and a hoarse cry starting earlier the previous evening and worsening overnight.

On examination he has audible inspiratory stridor at rest with mild intercostal recession but no tracheal tug and no nasal flaring. Air entry is symmetrical with no wheeze. He looks tired but engages with his parents and drinks half a cup of water in front of you. There is no drooling, no toxic appearance, and no history of a choking episode. Vaccinations including Haemophilus influenzae type b are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 140, blood pressure 96/58, respiratory rate 36, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 37.8 degrees

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer a single oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg dose and observe in the department with clinical reassessment after thirty minutes
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with only mild recession in a child who is alert, drinking, and not exhausted classifies as moderate croup, for which RCH recommends a single 0.15 mg/kg dose of oral dexamethasone with observation; the 0.6 mg/kg dose is reserved for severe croup. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Administer a single oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg dose and arrange immediate paediatric intensive care review by phone
options.rationale: The 0.6 mg/kg dose and intensive care notification are reserved for severe croup with marked recession, tracheal tug, exhaustion, or cyanosis. This child does not meet those criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and admit for a minimum four hour observation period after the dose
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for severe croup; mild and moderate disease do not require it. RCH specifies a minimum three hour observation period (not four) after the last nebulised adrenaline dose. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Discharge home on oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for three days with general practice review the next morning
options.rationale: Prednisolone has higher relapse and re-presentation rates than single dose dexamethasone for croup and is not preferred in the RCH protocol. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Arrange urgent retrieval to a tertiary centre for elective intubation and direct laryngoscopy in theatre
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure with exhaustion or cyanosis despite nebulised adrenaline. This child is alert, drinking, and saturating well. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup severity in Australian paediatric practice is graded by stridor pattern, work of breathing, and conscious state. Mild and moderate croup get a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg. Severe croup gets dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg plus nebulised adrenaline and intensive monitoring. After nebulised adrenaline, observe for at least three hours to detect rebound stridor before discharge.
key_points: Mild and moderate croup: oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose
Severe croup: oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg plus nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000
Minimum three hour observation after last nebulised adrenaline
Prednisolone is inferior to dexamethasone (higher relapse)
Drooling or stridor with toxic appearance suggests epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis instead
explanation.pearls: Stridor at rest with mild recession but a smiling drinking child is moderate croup and the correct dexamethasone dose is 0.15 mg/kg, not 0.6 mg/kg.

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to a metropolitan emergency department with 24 hours of watery diarrhoea up to ten times and three episodes of non-bilious vomiting. Her parents have offered sips of oral rehydration solution at home but she has refused most of it and only had one wet nappy in the last six hours.

On examination she is mildly drowsy but rouseable. Mucous membranes are tacky, eyes are slightly sunken, and capillary return is between two and three seconds peripherally. There is no abdominal distension, no peritonism, and no rash. She weighs 11 kg. There is no recent overseas travel, no antibiotic exposure, and no household member with similar symptoms beyond expected viral gastroenteritis circulating at her childcare.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 138, blood pressure 92/58, respiratory rate 28, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 37.6 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas within normal limits, capillary glucose 4.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in rehydration?

options.text: Insert two large bore intravenous cannulae and commence a 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9 percent saline over fifteen minutes followed by maintenance
options.rationale: Rapid intravenous bolus rehydration is reserved for severe dehydration with shock features (prolonged capillary return greater than three seconds, hypotension, depressed conscious state). This child has mild to moderate dehydration. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Discharge home on small frequent oral rehydration solution sips with safety netting advice and review by general practitioner within 24 hours
options.rationale: Home oral rehydration has already failed because of vomiting and refusal. Discharging without an in-department intervention risks ongoing decline and a repeat presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Administer a single 2 mg dose of oral ondansetron wafer and insert a nasogastric tube for rapid enteral rehydration over four hours
options.rationale: Single dose ondansetron (2 mg for 8 to 15 kg per RCH) reduces vomiting and admission rates. Nasogastric rehydration is first line for mild to moderate dehydration with failed oral therapy and is non-inferior to intravenous fluids while avoiding cannulation. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Commence oral loperamide 0.1 mg/kg three times daily and continue oral rehydration solution as tolerated at home
options.rationale: Anti-motility agents are contraindicated in paediatric gastroenteritis because of the risk of toxic megacolon and masking of bacterial colitis. They have no role in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Send stool for multiplex polymerase chain reaction and commence empirical oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for five days
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in suspected viral gastroenteritis without bloody stool, sepsis, or specific risk factors. Stool PCR does not change immediate rehydration management. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Paediatric gastroenteritis rehydration in Australia follows a stepped pathway based on dehydration severity. Mild to moderate dehydration that fails oral rehydration solution at home is treated in hospital with nasogastric rehydration as first line. A single dose of ondansetron facilitates oral or nasogastric tolerance. Intravenous fluids are reserved for severe dehydration, shock, or nasogastric failure.
key_points: Mild-moderate dehydration that fails oral: nasogastric rehydration is first line
Single dose oral ondansetron: 2 mg for 8 to 15 kg, 4 mg for 15 to 30 kg
Intravenous fluids only for severe dehydration, shock, or nasogastric failure
Anti-motility agents (loperamide) are contraindicated
Empirical antibiotics not indicated for routine viral gastroenteritis
explanation.pearls: Failed home oral rehydration solution plus moderate dehydration is the textbook indication for a single 2 mg ondansetron wafer plus nasogastric rehydration over four hours, not intravenous cannulation.

subtopic_detail: Anaphylaxis adrenaline dose by weight band
stem:
A 4-year-old girl is brought urgently to a metropolitan emergency department after eating a cashew biscuit at a birthday party. Within ten minutes her parents noticed lip swelling and a hoarse cry, and ambulance was called when she became wheezy and floppy in their arms en route.

On examination she is pale and lethargic with widespread urticaria and lip and tongue swelling. There is audible expiratory wheeze, mild intercostal recession, and a hoarse voice. Capillary return is three seconds and pulses are weak. She has no known prior reactions and no asthma diagnosis. Weight is documented as 16 kg from a clinic visit last week. There is no other ingestion and no recent infection. Intramuscular adrenaline has not yet been administered.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 168, blood pressure 78/40, respiratory rate 40, SpO2 91 percent on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate adrenaline dose?

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms per kilogram of 1:10000 solution into the deltoid muscle and repeat after five minutes
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis adrenaline is 1:1000, not 1:10000, and the ASCIA banded dosing table is preferred over per-kilogram arithmetic to reduce dosing error in the resuscitation environment. (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 500 micrograms of 1:1000 solution into the anterolateral thigh and repeat after ten minutes if needed
options.rationale: 500 micrograms (0.5 mL of 1:1000) is the adult and over 50 kg dose. A 16 kg child receives 150 micrograms by the ASCIA banded table. (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 1 microgram per kilogram of 1:10000 solution over one minute and titrate an infusion as required
options.rationale: Intravenous adrenaline is reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory anaphylaxis on a senior clinician decision. Intramuscular adrenaline is first line and faster to deliver. (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous adrenaline 300 micrograms of 1:1000 solution into the deltoid muscle and review response after five minutes
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline absorption is unreliable in anaphylaxis with peripheral shutdown; intramuscular into the anterolateral thigh is the recommended route. (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 150 micrograms of 1:1000 solution into the anterolateral thigh and repeat after five minutes if needed
options.rationale: The ASCIA banded paediatric dose for 10 to 20 kg is 150 micrograms (0.15 mL of 1:1000) intramuscular into the anterolateral thigh, repeatable every five minutes for ongoing reaction. (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis Clinical Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric anaphylaxis adrenaline dosing follows the ASCIA banded table by weight rather than per-kilogram arithmetic, which reduces calculation errors during resuscitation. The 10 to 20 kg band receives 150 micrograms of 1:1000 solution intramuscular into the anterolateral thigh, repeatable every five minutes. EpiPen Junior delivers this dose; EpiPen delivers 300 micrograms for 20 kg and above.
key_points: Use ASCIA banded adrenaline table, not mg/kg arithmetic
10-20 kg: 150 micrograms (EpiPen Jr); 20+ kg: 300 micrograms (EpiPen)
Adrenaline 1:1000, intramuscular, anterolateral thigh
Repeat every five minutes for ongoing reaction
Intravenous adrenaline reserved for arrest or refractory cases
explanation.pearls: For a 16 kg child in anaphylaxis the ASCIA banded dose is 150 micrograms of 1:1000 intramuscular, equivalent to an EpiPen Junior, not 0.16 mL of 1:1000 calculated by 10 micrograms per kg.

stem:
A 9-month-old boy is referred from a regional emergency department on day 7 of fever for second opinion. The fever has stayed between 38.6 and 39.4 degrees despite a five day course of oral cefalexin started in primary care. The parents report he is miserable, off feeds, and has a rash on his trunk and limbs that comes and goes.

On examination he is irritable but consolable. There is patchy non-blanching erythema on the trunk, mildly red cracked lips, and bilateral conjunctival injection sparing the limbus. Hands and feet are not swollen. There is a 1.2 cm right cervical lymph node. Throat is clean. There is no joint swelling, no organomegaly, and no peripheral peeling. He has no overseas travel and no known sick contacts.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 162, blood pressure 88/52, respiratory rate 34, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 39.1 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 96 g/L (105-135), platelets 642 x10^9/L (150-450), white cells 21.8 x10^9/L (5-15), CRP 168 mg/L (<5), ESR 84 mm/hr, albumin 26 g/L (35-50), ALT 102 U/L (<40), sodium 131 mmol/L (135-145), urinalysis pyuria with sterile culture, blood culture sterile at 48 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every six hours pending repeat blood cultures and review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Sterile blood cultures at 48 hours plus the constellation of fever, conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, and supportive laboratory findings argue against occult bacterial sepsis. Flucloxacillin would delay definitive treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over ten hours with oral aspirin 30 mg/kg per day in four divided doses
options.rationale: Two or three classical features (here three: conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash) plus supportive laboratory findings (CRP greater than 30, platelets greater than 450, anaemia, albumin less than 30, transaminitis, sterile pyuria) meets the American Heart Association incomplete Kawasaki criteria, particularly important under one year where coronary risk is highest. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Arrange same day outpatient echocardiography to look for coronary artery dilatation before deciding on intravenous immunoglobulin
options.rationale: Treatment for incomplete Kawasaki should not be delayed pending an echo result. Baseline echo is part of workup but treatment proceeds immediately when criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for three days as primary monotherapy ahead of immunoglobulin
options.rationale: Pulse methylprednisolone alone is not standard primary therapy for Kawasaki in Australia. Steroids are adjunctive in high risk cases or used for immunoglobulin resistance. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Continue supportive care with regular paracetamol and discharge home with paediatric clinic review in one week
options.rationale: Discharging without immunoglobulin in an infant under one year with incomplete Kawasaki criteria allows coronary artery inflammation to evolve unchecked; risk of aneurysm is highest in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is fever for five or more days with two or three classical features plus supportive laboratory findings. The diagnosis is particularly important under one year where coronary aneurysm risk is highest and classical features are most often incomplete. Australian practice mirrors the American Heart Association criteria with intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus high dose aspirin within ten days of fever onset.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki: fever 5 or more days plus 2-3 features plus supportive labs
Supportive labs: CRP > 30, platelets > 450, anaemia, albumin < 30, ALT raised, sterile pyuria
Highest aneurysm risk: infants under 1 year and over 8 years
Treatment: IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin 30-50 mg/kg per day during acute phase
Do not delay IVIG pending echocardiogram
explanation.pearls: A febrile infant under one year with at least two classical features and CRP above 30 plus albumin below 30 meets American Heart Association incomplete Kawasaki criteria and receives intravenous immunoglobulin immediately.

sources.label: American Heart Association Kawasaki Disease Scientific Statement 2017

stem:
A 21-day-old term boy is brought to a metropolitan emergency department after his mother noticed he felt warm and was harder to wake for feeds during the night. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks with maternal group B streptococcal swab negative at 36 weeks and no rupture of membranes risk factors.

On examination he is mottled and sleepy but rouseable with vigorous handling. He is poorly perfused with capillary return of four seconds. Fontanelle is soft. There is no rash, no respiratory distress, and no focal source of infection. His mother has no history of genital herpes and no febrile illness in the third trimester. The mother is not on any antibiotics and breastfeeds exclusively.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 184, blood pressure 64/38, respiratory rate 56, SpO2 96 percent on room air, temperature 38.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous gas pH 7.28 base excess negative 6, lactate 3.8 mmol/L, capillary glucose 3.2 mmol/L, urinalysis pending, point of care viral swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Take blood cultures, urine sample, and cerebrospinal fluid, then start intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin with a 20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin is the antimicrobial pair used during the first week of life. Beyond 7 to 28 days the empirical Australian regimen typically substitutes a third generation cephalosporin to cover community pathogens. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Take urine and blood cultures only, defer lumbar puncture, and start oral amoxicillin pending culture results in twenty four hours
options.rationale: Any unwell infant under 28 days requires full septic workup including lumbar puncture and parenteral empirical therapy. Oral amoxicillin is inadequate cover and deferring lumbar puncture is not appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Observe in the short stay unit with regular paracetamol and four hourly reviews to assess whether the temperature settles
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a febrile, poorly perfused 21-day-old with elevated lactate is unsafe. Bacterial sepsis and herpes simplex virus encephalitis can both progress rapidly without antimicrobials. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Take blood culture, urine, and cerebrospinal fluid then start intravenous cefotaxime, benzylpenicillin, and aciclovir with a 20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: An unwell febrile infant aged 7 to 28 days needs a full septic workup including lumbar puncture and immediate empirical cover for late onset group B streptococcal disease (benzylpenicillin), gram negative organisms and Listeria (cefotaxime), and herpes simplex encephalitis (aciclovir). Fluid resuscitation precedes inotropes. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Take blood cultures and urine then start intravenous ceftriaxone as monotherapy with a 10 mL/kg dextrose bolus to correct hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in the first month because it displaces bilirubin and can precipitate kernicterus. Cefotaxime is preferred. Monotherapy misses Listeria and herpes simplex virus, and dextrose alone does not address shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Any unwell febrile infant under 28 days receives a full septic workup (blood culture, urine, and cerebrospinal fluid for bacterial and herpes simplex virus studies) and immediate empirical broad spectrum antimicrobials. From 7 to 28 days the Australian regimen is intravenous cefotaxime plus benzylpenicillin to cover gram negatives, Listeria, and late onset group B streptococcus, plus aciclovir if there is any suggestion of meningitis or encephalitis.
key_points: Full septic workup including lumbar puncture in any unwell infant under 28 days
First week of life: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin empirically
7 to 28 days: cefotaxime plus benzylpenicillin empirically
Add aciclovir if seizures, encephalopathy, or HSV exposure risk
Ceftriaxone avoided in the first month (bilirubin displacement risk)
explanation.pearls: Substitute cefotaxime for ceftriaxone in the first month of life because ceftriaxone displaces bilirubin from albumin and precipitates kernicterus in the neonate.

stem:
A 5-week-old first-born boy is brought to a regional paediatric outpatient clinic with a four day history of forceful vomiting after every feed. The mother says the milk comes out in an arch and he is hungry again straight afterwards. His weight today is 4.0 kg compared with 4.3 kg at his two week check.

On examination he is alert but mildly dehydrated with sunken fontanelle and dry lips. Abdomen is soft with no palpable mass after careful palpation post-feed. Bowel sounds are present. There is no jaundice and no abdominal distension. There has been no bile-stained vomit, no haematemesis, and no diarrhoea. The mother is not on any medications and there is no family history of pyloric stenosis. He passes pale yellow urine in front of you.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 152, blood pressure 84/50, respiratory rate 38, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous gas pH 7.50 bicarbonate 32 mmol/L (22-28), sodium 136 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), chloride 94 mmol/L (95-110), capillary glucose 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent abdominal ultrasound to measure pylorus dimensions and admit for intravenous rehydration with correction of electrolytes
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis in a first-born male with projectile non-bilious vomiting at the typical age is classical for pyloric stenosis. Ultrasound is the diagnostic test (pyloric muscle thickness 3 mm or more, channel length 15 mm or more). The mass is often not palpable. Surgery only follows full correction of electrolytes. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Trial of thickened oral feeds for 48 hours and review in clinic if vomiting persists or weight gain remains poor
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic alkalosis with weight loss is not consistent with simple gastro-oesophageal reflux. Delay risks worsening dehydration and electrolyte derangement before urgent surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Send urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study to look for malrotation with volvulus and book the operating theatre
options.rationale: Malrotation with volvulus typically presents with bilious vomiting and is a surgical emergency with an upper gastrointestinal contrast study. This infant has non-bilious projectile vomiting and a metabolic picture pointing to pyloric stenosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting)

options.text: Commence oral omeprazole 1 mg/kg daily for suspected reflux oesophagitis and provide reassurance to the mother
options.rationale: Proton pump inhibitor treatment does not address mechanical gastric outlet obstruction and would delay diagnosis. Acid suppression is not the answer to projectile vomiting with weight loss. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Refer for surgical pyloromyotomy directly without imaging and start a slow intravenous infusion of 0.9 percent saline overnight
options.rationale: Ultrasound confirmation of pyloric stenosis is required before surgery, and pyloromyotomy is contraindicated until the metabolic alkalosis and hypokalaemia are corrected to avoid post-operative apnoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis is the most common surgical cause of vomiting in the first two months of life and classically presents in a first-born male with non-bilious projectile vomiting, weight loss, and hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Diagnosis is confirmed on ultrasound (pyloric muscle thickness 3 mm or more, channel length 15 mm or more). Pyloromyotomy is delayed until biochemistry is corrected to avoid post-operative apnoea from a respiratory compensation for ongoing alkalosis.
key_points: Classical presentation: first-born male, 3-8 weeks, non-bilious projectile vomiting
Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
Ultrasound: pyloric muscle thickness 3 mm or more, channel length 15 mm or more
Correct electrolytes BEFORE pyloromyotomy
Bilious vomiting at any age is malrotation until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Pyloromyotomy is deferred until bicarbonate is below 30 mmol/L and chloride is above 100 mmol/L because residual alkalosis drives post-operative apnoea through compensatory hypoventilation.
